ttps.description: Exploit public-facing cloud and web-adjacent services such as exposed Docker APIs, Kubernetes control planes, and Redis instances to gain initial access.
Use stolen or harvested credentials, SSH keys, cloud credentials, Kubernetes tokens, and wallet-related secrets for access and lateral movement.
Transfer malicious payloads through official release or CI/CD distribution channels to seed downstream infections.
Inhibit system recovery by wiping data on local hosts or Kubernetes nodes.

attack_chain: Compromise exposed cloud control planes and services
Harvest credentials and tokens from infected environments
Deploy wiper logic conditioned on locale/timezone checks
Destroy local data or cluster-wide data when Iran indicators are present

proposed_kb_patterns.attack_description: Abuse an exposed service that should be restricted by network controls or authentication, allowing the attacker to issue administrative actions without valid credentials.
proposed_kb_patterns.payload:
GET /containers/json HTTP/1.1
Host: TARGET:2375
Connection: close
detect: Docker engine metadata or container IDs returned

proposed_kb_patterns.attack_description: Query a Kubernetes API endpoint that is exposed without proper authentication or authorization, enabling enumeration of cluster resources and follow-on destructive actions.
proposed_kb_patterns.payload:
GET /api/v1/nodes HTTP/1.1
Host: TARGET:6443
Authorization: Bearer invalid
Connection: close
detect: node objects returned despite invalid token

proposed_kb_patterns.attack_description: Access a Redis instance with no authentication or weak network controls and leverage server commands to inspect or alter persistence-related state.
If a web app or automation wrapper renders attacker-controlled strings into a template context, use expression syntax to trigger server-side evaluation and confirm code execution context.
